CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We have been round the country a good deal during
the past few months. There is much talk about: the
brightening of rural life and something is being done.
But what of urban life? Some time ago we thought we
might have to put up for a night in a certain town of
more than 1,000 inhabitants, and our heart sank at the
prospect. Fow would we pub in the evening and the
night until bed-time? Rural life wants brightening,
but urban life is in need of a good shaking up, and of
a generous treatment of constructive reform. An out-
sider’s impression of a town may make interesting read-
ing, bub it scarcely conduces to anything constructive.
The clever men who know any particular town, who

_know its needs, difficulties and possibilities, are the

)le who have things to say that might lead up to the |

stimulate energy in a legitimate way for the benefit of
the country,.and induce practical, level-headed people
to let us have their views; if later on we find by ex-
perience that the naming of a particular town would
be a good plan we will adopt it.

There are few towns in Ireland that wouldn’t be the
better materially and socially of turning their minds
back on themselves and considering seriously their pos-
sibilities. If we can stimulate men of practical ideas
to write articles that will be published in the LEADER,
the particular towns would have the grey matter of
their brains stirred up and surplus intellectual energy
mizht possibly spend itself in the columns of the local
papers, and much in the nature of the verb ‘“ to do’*
might conceivably result. We are not accustomed to
let fancy run away with us, and it may be that many
a reader living in a town who reads these lines will say
from his heart—‘‘ Improve my town, the only thing T
could suggest would be to burn it, agup na oaome tei”

Even so, there may be others who take a more hopeful
and masculine view of things. There is Kilkenny.
Captain Cuffe has done comparatively wonders there,
but are there not a lot of things the people could, by
stirring themselves, do for themselves? We believe we
mortally offended some kind people in Clonakilty, be-
cause we called it a ramshackle old place; we are sorry
that we hurt the susceptibilities of any inhabitant of the
town of the famous ‘‘ Rastler ”’; but we cannot con-
scientiously withdraw the word ‘‘ ramshackle.”” Surely
much could be done if only the people of Clonakilty
would stir themselves, and, having braced themselves
up to the verb ““ to do,’”” go ahead and make urban his-
tory. We write this way because we believe firmly in
the future of Ireland, urban and rural. We recall a
few other towns we have heen in comparatively
recently—Dunmanway, Fermoy, Clones, Athenry, Tul-
lamore, Drogheda, Mallow, Tipperary, Cashel, Thurles,
Armagh, Kildare, Clonmel, Naas, Wicklow, and so on.
All these towns and scores of others could add to their
stature by taking thought. The people of the urban
centros and of the big villages must bustle and look
alive if they want to get on; and the more they shake
themselves up the better. ~We hope our readers who

‘have level heads and can put their views on paper will

help us in our endeavour to shake up the towns. We
lay down little or no conditions. ~We considered
whether politics should be barred, but we don’t believe
in barring anything except what is wrong.  Leb an
honest man look out on his town with his own eyes and
temper his vision with his judgment and send us the
result. To the writer of the first published article on
any particular town we will send a guinea, and a half-
guinea to the writer of .any other article on the same
town that we may publish. These offers are only by

way of stimulating writers, for probably most of the

- addresses in confidence.

men and women who have views to offer would not be
induced to offer them merely for the modest reward;
‘but there are many people wiho want a sort of objective
or inducement before them. They would go for a walk
if: they only had a letter to post or a penny lead-pencil
to buy, but otherwise they would not stir out. We
hope that our suggestion will arouse many clever buf
easy-going people to give the country and their fellow
townsmen the benefit of their ideas. ~Writers can use a
pen-name, but of course we must have their names and
a We are on the track of heal-
ing suggestions, rather than fulminations; we don’t
want libellous matter as it is often costly ; and we may
also state that when an article goes woll over a page—
-a page of the LEADER runs to about 1,500 words—we
begin to calculate how many per cent. of our readers
decide to skip it.

There was a great Flome Rule demonstration at Newry
recently. There were ten resolutions proposed, Nos. &
and 8 of which read, respectively, as follows: —

6. “That, as the Irish language is the birthright of our nation,
and as it is the most distinct mark of our Nationality, we pledge
our.elves to further the movement for the restoration of the Irish
Ianguage to its rightful placo as the national languago of our
couniry by giving it our practical support; and wo call upon the
Zeaders cf the Irish Party and the National Organisation to make
Irish their official language for the transaction of all their busi-
ness as soon as practicable.”

8. “That in order to stem the ever-increasing tide of em
which is robbing Ireland annually of thousands ot the and
Eealthizst of her. children, we consider it absolutely necessary that
the Trish people should give their whole-hearted support to articles
of Irish manufacture, and we call upon the merchants and shop-
keepers to stock Irish goods, and give same cvery prommence in
their establishments.”

tion,

It is a forward sign to see that even a resolution at a
political Tome Rule meeting calls upon the leaders of
the Irish Party and the ‘“ National Organisation’’ to
make Irish their official language for the transaction of
all their business as soon as practicable. Young Mr.
Mooney, the publican, is M.P. for Newry, and 1t will
be remembered that about seven years ago he wrote an
““ emphatic yes’’ across some questions put to him
by the Gaelic League at the time he was seeking the
favour of South Dublin. Since the time of writing the
‘‘ emphatic yes’’ we are not aware that he has done
anything further by way of helping the language move-
ment ; we have no information to enable us to say that
even bo agur ca would not be foreign words to him.
Mr. Mooney, in the course of his speech, did not refer
to the resolution about the Irish language.

Neither did Mr. Joe Devlin, though he referred to
““their distinguished representative, Mr. Mooney.”
Neither Mr. Mooney nor Mr. Devlin said anyth'ng
about the 8th resolution concerning the support of Irish
manufactured goods. The John Mitchell Branch of
the Irish National Foresters, however, presented an ad-
dress in the Irish language. Needless to say, neifher
Mr. Mooney nor Mr. Devlin replied to it in Trish. We
would not press the point against either gentleman that
he was not able to make an eloquent speech in Irish,
but all Irish Nationalists ought by this time be able to
say a few sentences in Irish in public. But what do
Messrs. Mooney or Devlin care about the Trish lan-
guage? As the Foresters presented their address in
Irish, why did the Hibernians and why did the Newry
Urban Council present their addresses in English? The
treasurer of the local branch of the Gaelic League is
also a member of the Urban Council.

reference to a question raised in these 'co}umus, as to

We have received a letter from an anonymous corre-
spondent re Oscott College. Surely our correspondent
ought to know that he ought to have given us his name
and address in confidence. Ile makes several state-
ments which we have no doubt are true, for we helieve
the letter is genuine; but supposing the letter was a
hoax and {(he statements were inventions, how pretty we
would leok hefore the public when we explained that
we allowed the statements into  our paper on the
strength of an anonymous letter. With the argument
in the letter we can deal. The writer objects to our
calling Oscott ‘‘tony’’; he says that “the epithet is
as uncalled for as it is undeserved.”” Well, then, Oscott
is not ‘‘tony.” We know little of Oscott beyond its
name, and we called it ““ tony ’’ because ‘ One Finger
Jack >’ sent his illustrious son, ‘“ Ragamuffin Fitz,”’ of
Ashpot notoriety, to it. If it isn’t, or wasn’t, *‘ tony,”’
well, then, probably ‘‘ One Finger Jack > committed a
great error of judgment from his point of view. We
take it that men like the late ‘“ One Finger Jack ’’ and
his kind send their sons to Oscott, Beaumont, Stoney-
hurst, and such places in order that they may mix
about with h-English boys, get up a h-accent and be-
come h-eligible for the *“ Freebooters *’ when they come
back to their native dunghill called *“ I-ah-land.”  Now,
if the term ‘ tony,”” as applied to Oscott, is an epithet,
““as uncalled for as it is undeserved,”” it would appear
that the Trish Seo:nin parents who sent their sons there
rather than to St. Colman’s College, Fermoy, Rockwell,
Clongowes, Blackrock, etc., made fools of themselves as
well as doing an economic injury to their country. Our
anonymous correspondent says that Sir Thomas
Esmonde was educated there. We mnever met Sir
Thomas Esmonde, but notwithstanding his political
vagaries we have a warm regard for him as an Irish
public man; but perhaps he would have a better in-
sight into things had he been educated in a College in
Ireland. Tt appears that in 1889 Oscott was closed to
lay students, and since then is an exclusively clerical
institution. We have no name and address behind this
statement, so it- must be accepted at readers’ risk.

Take the cases supplied by some of our large towns and cities

At a recent meeting of Convocation of the Royal
University Mr. Michael Sullivan was voted to the chair.
It would appear that there were less present than the
minimum thirty necessary to form a quorum.  Mr.
Michael Sullivan was in the act of making his remarks
when Sir James Creed Meredith, one of the highly-paid
secretaries of the Royal University, and likewise Deputy
Grand Master of the pernicious secret society known as
the Freemasons in Ireland, rose to a point of order.
The Chairman, Mr. Michael Sullivan, said to the
Deputy Grand Master of the secret society of Free-
masons in Ireland, ‘ Don’t make a speech.”” 1T only
want,”” replied this member of a pernicious secret
society, ‘“T only want to reply to observations made by
you in reference to matters——’’ ™I call upon you to
sit down,”” said Mr. Sullivan to the Freemason. And
Mr. Sullivan added, ‘“ If you stand up to make a speech
you are out of order. Sit down, sir.” That’s the
way to treat these bumptious and disorderly persons
like Sir James Creed Meredith. IIow the Deputy
Grand Master of Freemasons in Ireland must have
winced atb being ordered to ‘“ Sit down, sir,”’ by a mere
gritty “ Idolator.” :

Irish Manufactured Altar Wax Candles.—Mr. P.
O’Connor, 23 D’Olier Street, writes us that he i
informed some persons are spreading reports
through the country that he is not a manufacturer of
Altar Wax Candles. As Mr. O’Connor, in his adver-
tisement and circulars, freely invites all clergymen to
inspect his factory, an invitation which a great many
have accepted and which we also have availed ourselves
of, we are surprised how anyone can dare to circulate
these untruthful reports. Mr. O’Connor will feel thanlk-
ful to anyone giving him information regarding the
author of these falsehoods.

We see by Pink that during last week, when Dirty
Dublin were flocking to the Tanqueray and other exhibi-
tions of the Gaiety, the band, amongst other things,
played ‘‘Liszt’s Hungarian Rapsody.”” Has this any
connection with the ‘Green Hungarian Band’’%
‘“ Lisat’s Hungarian Rapsody ’’ sounds rather familiar
to us. Where did we read of it, or is it only a dream?:

Pink also tells us that ‘‘ the ’cello solo was beautifully
rendered by our local rising young artist, S. Rosen-
berg.” It does not strike us that there is very muck
local about Rosenberg in these latitudes.

We see that Mr. Harvey is coming from Britain, if
he has not already come, to entertain some Dublin
people—for we still import our amusements to a great
oxtent. He is to play ‘“ The Breed of the Treshams.”
One would have thought that this imported play had
been already threshed for all it was worth.

By the way, why does not the Dublin Industrial Asso-
ciation wait on the Theatre Royal and the Gaiety, and
ask them to cease importing entertainments for the
Dublin people. . !

of the Pink-Euvening Telegraph, topped by the twe
sprightly damsels kicking up their heels that * adorn >
the  Music and Drama *’ puffs in Saturday’s issues.

coming to the ““ h-Empire >’ music hall, and says: * The

Mr. W. B. Yeats is to produce a “‘ new ’’ play called
* Deirdre.”” W. B. appears to be fond of burnishing
up his old plays to look like new ones. There was a
‘“Deirdre > by W. B. played last November, and we
went to the playhouse to appreciate, but remained to
scoff. There was a lady—she was specially imported for
the occasion, if we remember aright—and she took the
part of Deirdre. At the time we remarked: ‘‘The
“Deirdre’ of Miss Darragh belonged rather to the
‘ atmosphere ’ of Mr. Pinero’s plays than to that of
Pagan Ireland.”” And now it is announced that the
new ‘‘ Deirdre,”” which we suppose will be the old one
painted up and .with her hair in curls, is to be played
by the British lady who delighted Dirty Dublin in the
Tanqueray abomination. Mr. Yeats took some part in an
anti-vandalism affair concerning Tara hill some years
ago. Why dig up Deirdre out of the storied past and
bracket her, as it were, with ‘ The Second Mrs. Tan-
queray ’? W. B. is very shrewd; he only plays being
the “ gom.”” He may have concluded that such things
as ‘‘ Deirdre *’ won’t take on with Dirty Dublin so long
as the impression holds that they concern anything so
low from Merrion Square’s point of view as ancient Ire-
land ; if the ‘“ Deirdre * play could be made to fix itself
in Merrion Square’s mind as being part and parcel
of the ““ woman with a past’’ sort of -thing, Merrion
Square might go to see it. =~ W. B. should call his
““new ’’ play ‘‘ The Second Mrs. Deirdre! ’

Tralee Petty Sessions there were two charges of
drunkenness against some man who produced a
pledge bearing an Irish signature. This Mr.
P. T. Foley, as reported in the local press, said:
““ We speak English in this court, and we cannot take
anything that’s signed in Irish.”” What ho! Mr. P.
English Foley! Probably there is a good deal of in-
different and broken English spoken on the
bench of the Tralee Petty Sessions, but even if the Jay
Pees were all Addisons, so far as their English was con-
cerned, it does not at all follow that they should reject
a document signed in Irish. That is merely ignorance,
and Mr. P. English Foley, J.P., of Tralee, writes, or
speaks, himself down a very ignorant man. If a docu-
ment written and signed in French, where the nun

lish Foley, J.P., would spend half the night over a
French-English dictionary in a wild endeavour to appear
as one capable of reading ‘‘ Frinch”’ on the ‘‘ Binch

A PLEA FOR IRISH LETTERS.

0! steal not the sweetest of all the dear charms
That are round Ireland’s language to-day ;

1t will ne’er be the same to the heart of the Gael,

" If you take its quaint lotbers away.

Lob it be as it was in the days Llmtf are gone,
Tt the learning be stifl if it will,

Bub the fight that is won against heaviest odds,
Makes the victory more glorious still.

Oh! doubt not if changes are made in our tongue,
And the pathway of learning too clear;

It is then that the love of the Gael will grow cold,
And the language of Ireland less dear.

It changed not through ages of darkness and tears,
"Tis as pure and as bright as before,

And now, as we’re saving this link to the past,
Why not leave it the same as of yore?

A correspondent from Newcastle West informs us that

ment from the Newcastle West Union for English-made
mattresses has excited a good deal of abiention and
curiosity. It is generally believed that there is a stand-

FRUIT FOR JOHN BULL AND OURSELVES.

DEear Sir—I am replying to Mr. R. Walsh per post,
but for the benefit of ofher readers of the LEADER whe
may think T was only joking in stating that John Bull
was willing to give £14 per ton for gooseberries, I wish
to say that I was perfectly serious. I have in my pos-
session a letter from a cross-Channel fruit salesman
offering £14 a ton for the fruit. The letter was written
last year, and I believe the price is not as good this
season, but I am informed by one'of the Departmeny’s
horticultural instructors that not only £14, but as high
as £17 a ton can be got, and has been got, for goose-
berries. I may add tthat as an acre of ground may be
got to produce 4 tons of the fruit, that quantity, at as
low as even £7 per ton, ought to remunerate a grower
fairly. I advert to the point just to strengthen the
arguments as to the money to be made by fruit-growing,
set out in my original article.

ANOTHER DAY AT KILSKEERY.

I DEALT last week with my first evening’s experience

in the parish of Kilskeery. The next morning
Father Maguire brought me down to one of the Trillick
National Schools—the one that he is manager of, the
other is a non-Catholic school, and so far Irish is not
taught in it. Trillick school in the morning was occu-
pied largely by mere mites—but bright little people.
It is a mixed school, that is, there are children of both
sexes in it. A volley of pLdn Leac, pldén Leat came
after me as I left the school, the mites became excitedly
articulate as soon as the visitor’s back was turned to
leave. I had a walk up the village of Trillick and went
into the post office for a while. The post office, or the
shop in which the post office is situate, is also the
stationery and book depdt of Trillick. The books, so
far as I could see, are largely Irish and Irish-Ireland
books, and there was quite an exceptionally large stock
of them.

From a hill south of the village there is a splendid
view as Trillick sends its chimney smoke up from the
hollow below in the morning light ; over the way, beyond
the village, is the Hill of the Fosts, where the great
Feis was held last August. There is a wide expanse of
hilly, undulating country visible, and one notices that,
comparatively speaking, a large proportion of the land is
under tillage. I understand that nearly all of the land
in the parish has been bought out.

On the way back through the village Father Maguire
introduced me to Miss Barry, the Domestic Economy
and Cookery Instructress, who works under the Com-
mitteo for Home Brightening, of which I will deal
later. We went back to inspect the Trillick School
Garden, where the boys are taught to grow the veget-
ables that Miss Barry teaches their sisters to cook. The
School Garden in Trillick, like the other three school
gurdens in the parish, is & new institution. It is about
% an acre in area, and it was in grass as late as Mavch last.

* A Gardening Instructor, Mr. Kavanagh, was engaged

in connection with the Flome Brightening Scheme
operating in the parish, and he began his work in April
and only finished in October. In the Trillick garden
there are, or were, from 15 to 20 of the hardier kinds

4 NbVEMBER'2, 1907.

of vegetables, and from 6 to 10 of the hardier kinds of

flewers; and bhe girls, particularly in connection with

the flowers, come in for some of the instruction. Mr.

o McS_m‘ley, N.T., kindly showed me round the garden
fmd Ppointed with pride to some of the wonderful turnips
in the ground. There were curly cabbage, leeks, brock-
ley, parsnips, carrots, lettuce, etc. The ground was
deeply trenched, and a few walks made through it in
the early stages; and the fencing also was part of the
initial trouble. ~ There are four school gardens in the
parish, one of which—at Golan—I saw by moonlight the
previous night. The others are at Knocknagor and Kil-
skeery.

The National School of Kilskeery is adjacent to the
parish church. A little girl of nine—T afterwards was
informed that she was only seven, and whicn story is
correct I cannot say, probably the latter—Mary
Catherine Gallagher sat up on a stool and told a- story
in Irish with excellent d/as and great self-possession.
This little child knew no Irish about a yoar previously,
so that she is a conerete argument on the Irish language
side so far as children in English-speaking parishes are
concerned. And other little people in the parish, I
was informed, could tell stories also, but I only write of
what I witnessed. The schoolmaster at Kilskeery, Mr.
John McBride, has recently qualified under the Board
for Trish Teaching. The National schoolmasters have
to keep a lot of irons in the fire, and it is a great shame
that they are not better paid. Here is a teacher of over
twenty years’ service, whose efficiency has been guaran-
teed by over half a dozen inspectors—some of them head
inspectors—deprived of first grade, and even of the
wretched increments, because, owing to uncontrollable
-circumstances, the average of Kilskeery school is below
that required by the inexorable National Board. Three
little girls from the class in Kilskeery obliged with a
xeel, the master, Mr. McBride, supplying the music.

The parish church at Kilskeery is a plain, indeed a
very plain, but roomy huilding.  There is not one
‘thing in it, or about it, to hurt the ‘‘ economic sense ’’
-of the most captious critic. Yet withal there is a de-
~vctional atmosphere about it, with its slates and rafters
visible from the inside. Tt is a country church of the
old times when people felt thankful that they had any
sort of a church at all. Tt is in a hollow ; so very much
in a hollow that in rainy times floods interfere with the
approach to it. The Irishisation of Kilskeery will, in

:good time no doubt, involve a new church'on a new and
mcre convenient site for the parish. Father Maguire
spcke to me of Spiddal church, though he had nof yet
seen it, but he will probably have a look at it before the
time comes for seriously considering the building of a
mew church in his parish. In the meantime, should this
creach the eyes—as probably it will—of natives of
Kilskeery now living in other parts of Ireland, in
‘America, and various foreign parts, they might take a
note of this want of their native parish. As I am re-
‘referring to Kilskeery wants I may as well say that a
“Parish Hall is badly nceded also, so that the Irish forces
‘might have a rallying centre for social and intellectual
life.  Miss Alice Milligan visited the parish last Christ-
mas and entertained it with lantern lectures in Irish
“history, but owing to the want of a Parish Hall she had
*to lecture in six different centres. 1

In the carly evening Miss Barry conducted a cookery
~class at Trillick school.' The girls turned up in great
force, bub I became painfully conscious of my lack of
ability as an amateur school inspector at this cookery
-class.” The proof of the cookery is in the eating, so far
.as I.am concerned, and the science and practice of pots
and' pans is beyond me. One of the-demonstrations was

-the cooking of an oatmeal pudding, sufficient for four

people, at a cost of 24d.; I saw a cauliflower also laid
out cooked in excellént style. But, though I have to

‘skip these matters lightly, feeling that the loss'I say

about, them the less of my ignorance on such an im-

portant branch of the domestic axts I will display, it is
well for the readers to know that Miss Barry, an ex-
professor of domestic science at the Kildare Street
Demestic Scieace College, Dublin, has charge of this
branch of the many-sided, progressive and Irish work
now going on in the parish. 5 3

After cookery there came aceilro. The parish dipped
in in ones and twos and threes, and Father ITackett,
C.C., from Fintona, and Father Quiglcy,'C.C.,‘ from
Enniskillen, who came over to Trillick specially on TFather
Maguire’s invitation, were also present. Our readers,
have already been told that Mr. Philip \Valdron,.of
Ballyhaunis, a native speaker, is one of the Irish
teachors in the parish. ~Mr. Waldron, founder of the
Ballyhaunis Branch of the Gaelic League, and for
several years iis efficient teacher; secretary again
and again of the Mayo Feis, first prize winner at
various Feiseanna and repeatedly at the Oipeactap in
literary and other competitions, author of the Irish
Ode of Welcome,” recently read to Dr. Hyde on the
oceasion of his surprise visit to Partry, has found in
Kilskeery a congenial sphere for his vast Irish-Treland
activities. Father Maguire has also secured the ser-
vices of another native speaking certificated teacher, and
a distinguished Oireachtas prize-winner for three years,
Miss Annie Hession, from Belclare, Co. Galway. She
teaches Irish and traditional singing in the day and
evening schools, and teaches the Kilskeery Trish Choir,
who sing Irish hymns on Sundays. The ceilio was
started with €ibuin & pam by the choir, Miss Hession
conducting. Two little children sang i o guiun uipin Tu
and the chorus was taken up with great spirit.  Miss
Hession put the girls of the cookery class through
their Irish, and the girls appeared to be every bit as
gaood as the boys and men at the language.  During
an interval between traditional singing and a six-hand
reel I inspected some crochet work, a blouse, and a
lady’s dress that were made in the parish, for there is
1o effort being spared to bring up the girls handy and
useful as well as graceful Irish dancers and fluent Irish
speakers.  Master Dan M‘Elholm, a graceful exponent
of traditional Irish step-dancing, and his sister danced .a
double jig faultlessly, afterwards Dan executed a

hornpipe with characteristic grace. Then a little
girl, Rosanna Breen, delivered with a fine ovldp
an Irish  recitation. One of the most
popular items of the ceiio was the singing

by Miss Hession of ‘ Roudalam Randy,’’ and I can tell
you nearly all the Kilskeerians, young and old, that
were in Trillick School that night joined in the chorus.
About twenty of the girls danced ‘“ The Waves of
Tory,”” and the rallying song of the Gaelic League
wound up the festive part of the evening’s proceedings.
Before Father Maguire and I left, and when the specta-
tors had gone, it was after seven o’clock in the evening,
and a class of about 40, mostly composed of full-grown
people, sat down with their books in their hands to an
hour’s Irish lesson from Mr .Waldron.

It was after nine o’clock when Father Maguire and I
reached the night school at Knocknagor, in a far away
corner of the parish. Here again there were about forty
students nearly all grown men and only a few young-
sters. The class was in full swing, and the animation
and brightness of the students contrasted strongly with
the antiquated school; a new school is badly needed at
Knocknagor. The class was in charge of Mr. Grainger,
and the master, Mr. John Colten, has qualified under
the National Board as a teacher of Irish. This visit
practically marked the end of my inspection of Irish
Kilskeery, for the next day (IFriday) was utterly wet
and I did not, go anywhere.

I have so far given a sketch of what T .observed.
Noxt week I hope to-finish with Kilskeery by writing
the results of reflection as distinct from those of obser-
vation, setting forth Father Maguire’s plans-and his
means of carrying them out, and showing in broad out-
line what might be done for Irish-Treland in every
parish in the English-speaking districts.

tales and poems enlivens the book and frees it from that
prosiness consequent on a series of articles on such a
subject as the abuse of alcohol. Some of the lyrics are
3 from the pen of Canon Casey—the bard of the Suck, a

Llaogape ; a poem in the same language by .dn
€pao bin 5 some tales of real life from memoirs of
Father Mathew; an amusing tale of a toper; drink
anecdotes—some sad and terrible; and a tragic tale by
the wife of an inebriate giving a lurid picture of the life
a girl may look forward to who intends marrying a
drunkard. When one thinks of the numbers of women
throughout Ireland ‘‘ watching ’’ himself on fair days,
high days and holidays in the vain hope of preventing
him from liquefying the cash so badly needed to fill
their children’s mouths and clothe their young limbs
decently, one sincerely wishes that ‘“ The Father Mathew
Temperance Reader ’’ might be in the hands of every
young girl to stimulate thought, and make her look

before she leaps into the home of a drunkard.

The evil effects of drink is, in this little book, treated
from every point of view—in regard to the home, the
nation, the children, health, trade, industry, emigra-
tion ; and last, but not least, woman as its victim and
apostle. Father Halpin leaves no side of this dread
curse untouched. In beautiful, yet simple, language he
draws a picture of the home, to wit:—

‘““ How beautiful is that simple word Home! What
sweet and sacred memories does it recall—of youth, its
innocence and pleasures, of parents and their affections,

i . of love and devotion, of union and peace. The home
may be broken up, and its members scattered to many
lands; youth may have grown to manhood, and man-

l is ever present; and, to the last, the mind will wander
back to the old familiar scenes and associations of youth.
But—home is something more than this, and, as the
homes are, so shall the nation be.”” ;

The above is a volume in itself, for surely in no
country in the world has the word ‘‘ home ’’ missed its
real meaning more than in Ireland. A stranger passing
by would pause and wonder what curse is on the land
until he sees men—gaunt and half-starved, maybe—
staggering homeward from fair or market when the
truth hits him in the face. It is the curse of Drink—

Father Halpin’s chivalty gets the - better of his

the children.

< At the entrance of life stands the mother.”  No

without making up her mind then and there to be
henceforth, not only a total abstainer; but a strenuous
apostle of temperance; and women can do much in this
way—if they only try. .

The chapter on woman’s work for temperance is excel-
lent reading.

“TIn order to attack vice with effect we must set up
something better in its place,”’ he quotes, and then he
goes on to enumerate the many ways in which a woman
may pit her strength against this deadly vice, to wit—
to disillusion herself of the fallacy that alcohol has any
medicinal properties, that it is other than a poison, slow
if you will, but still a poison, in sickness or health; to
have by her books on cookery, sick-room management,
domestic economy and thrift; to acquire a knowledge of S

‘“ There is a rivalry between the home and the public-

connection with alcohol are treated exhaustively. To
quote :—

‘“Of the profits derived from the manufacture of
drink, the Government takes 58 per cent., the distiller
14} per cent., the publican 24§ per cent., and the
worker a miserable 3 per cent. In other words, of a
profit of, say, 2s. 4d.—the price usually paid over the -

share in the drink trade is about 1s. 6d. in the pound,

I might quote a great deal more from this excellent
and useful book, but it is not easy to pick out gems
where all shine. _Suffice it to say that it supplies a long-
felt want in the National Schools, it is suitable for young

ranch system through these Returns. These evils are
well epitomised in the celebrated lines of Goldsmith: —

WRITER in the ¢ Termes’’ speaks of the
& vigorous truthfulness of the LEADER. The term
strikes one as most apt, for what we need in Ireland is
not mere pallid anzmic truth, but truthfulness vigor-
ously pushed home, even when a llttlp lmpfxlatable.
That is how the LeADER has become a stimulating force
in Trish public life. 3

If you get a Thom’s Directory, and glance at a list
of the Catholic clergy of the Diocese of Dublin, you will
find that some thirty are graduates of the Royal Univer-
sity. And again, if you glance at the list of members of
Convocation who were present at the Royal University,
under the chairmanship of Mr. M. Sullivan, LL.B., on
Tuesday last, I do not think that you will find the name
of a single secular priess. Yet the principal business
was to request the Government to settle the University
question this-session, and a unique opportunity offered
for pressing the claim for a Catholic University, if only
the natural supporters of such a step had troubled te
put in an appearance.

Of course, many of the Dublin clergy who are Royal
University graduates are not qualified to vote in Convo-
cation.  But this is their own fault. The Catholic
Defence Society addressed a circular to them last year,
pressing on them to join, yet the response was very poor.
It is true that a few of them who are members attend
very well, but on this occasion the only clerical repre-
sentatives were five or six Jesuits and a couple of Fran-
ciscans.

Why one complains of the absence of the secular clergy
